item.title: further testing warranted
item.content:encoded:
If I were a foundation researcher, I would want to genetically analyze the potatoes, as well as try growing some of them and breeding them with regular potatoes, to see what happens.

Nevermind, just read the exploration log. Introducing any of these supertubers to our world without the intention to immediately cook and eat them would be a dumb idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What a great SCP. The absurdity of a world filled entirely with potatoes does not compromise the article's serious tone, and even a much more formal exploration log still keeps it grounded.

+1.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0.

The second image comes from here, without an explicit Creative Commons license.

Both images have since been removed.

potatoes
potatoes

potatoes
poTAtoes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mekhane, Yaldabaoth, the entire pantheon of the SCPverse be damned, the world behind the potato sack was completely wiped out by their version of the Keter Cakes.

300 billion kg worth of potatoes on earth. Just considering density, that's 300 million cubic meters. This volume doubles every 2 hours. To fill up the known universe, 3.58 × 10^80 cubic meters, it will take 478 hours. 20 days.

(I dont know mathematically how much space between potato shaped 3d objects would form when they are all next to each other But I guess I'll ignore that for now.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here after seeing it on The Volgun's channel.

I gotta say I'm genuinely impressed with this SCP. It takes an idea that is first weird and funny, and gradually turns it into something dreadful and scary. Two main things that made me like it so much:

1: The implication that the pocket dimension the potatoes come from is a world just like ours, where something went very, very wrong.

2: The fact that the agents didn't die at the end of the event log, for once. It happens so often on this site that reading one where they actually get out shaken but unscathed is refreshing.

So, I'm giving this an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I would be fascinated to learn what, exactly, happened in the other world that led to this. I'm thinking something similar to that cake scp whose number escapes me at the moment. Potatoes just multiplied exponentially until everyone was literally crushed by food.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the story potential of the alternate world on the other side: Some scientists thought they ended world hunger by inventing a potato that could duplicate itself, and it worked! For a while anyway. World hunger was deemed solved, and the scientists responsible were heroes. But as time went by, people realized that the spuds were multiplying faster than the poor were eating them. As people got more nervous, they let out a special announcement that everyone had to start eating more potatoes, poor or not. They start incinerating them, sending them to space, but nothing they could do was enough. Eventually, humanity was crushed by the literal weight of their own genius.
And the bag is just a little window into that world they left behind.
Boom. Tale.

Oh, also, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hahahahahahahahaha
*reads log*
Holy fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really, if you have infinite potatoes, and you eat an infinite amount, there will still be potatoes. The potatoes left will ferment, so you will then get infinite vodka. There's no reason you can't have both, because you will never run out of either, resolving the debate between the Irish and the Russians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And here we have the Irish/Russian Dilemma: Do we eat the infinite potatoes now, or do we wait for them to ferment to infinitely drink them later?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is easily my favourite SCP on this entire site, it's silly, well written, intriguing, and yet slightly creepy at the same time (The bicycle, oh god). The exploration log is what really makes it, it adds much more person detail than most SCPs do out there.
I also love the idea of a bunch of serious-faced SCP personnel climbing into a bag of potatoes one at a time.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Such a jaded team captain. His report definitely made for a hilarious read. Plus the very unusual situation of being trapped in a bag of tubers made for an interesting read in and of itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was looking for a really scary SCP to read when I caught a glimpse of this and thought, "why not?". I was not disappointed; it was really funny. The exploration log is what makes it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why potatoes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except in SCP-verse these things ALWAYS go wrong… ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
please see Addendum-1, Exploration Log 1689-I.

Shouldn't that be "Addendum-2"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hum… why do they assume nobody reads exploration logs and if he's right, why does no-one read them? Why would the Foundation bother with exploration missions if they don't check the logs?!

Simple: could be their perception of things. Or, they don't actually look at the exploration logs too much unless something goes wrong. The latter seems more likely. At my job as a Security Officer, we are required to fill out a Daily Action Report and a Vehicle Condition Log. Waste of time, as we know for a fact that nobody checks them… Until something goes wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't sure about this as it seemed like just another "infinite supply of whatever" SCP, complete with the possibility of it taking over the World if left unchecked, but the exploration log definitely sold me on it. It's written efficiently and it's genuinely creepy. However, I do have a couple nitpicks:

"this is supposed to be an official log, so I'm supposed to call them Agents Meryle, Ozols, and Peterson. But no one checks these. So I fucking won't."

Hum… why do they assume nobody reads exploration logs and if he's right, why does no-one read them? Why would the Foundation bother with exploration missions if they don't check the logs?!

"Turns out, about a hundred meters of our tunnel is caved in."

How do they know it's "about a hundred meters"? They obviously have no way to measure it.

"My taste buds are starting to shut down."

He's been on an all-potatoes diet for two days and his taste buds are already shutting down? Yes, I know he's probably being metaphorical but still, you'd think an elite agent would be able to stomach sub-par meals for a couple days.

I did like it a lot overall though and I especially appreciate the fact that unlike most similar companion pieces, the explorers do survive and even recover. Even the more humorous moments don't feel out of place and you can believe these people in this situation to be acting the way they do.

item.title: placenames
item.content:encoded: Turns out there really is a Krysovo, but it's in Udmurtia. Should we change the name again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I didn't know why this was rated so high. It just felt really bland. Then, I read the exploration log.

I never thought I'd be scared of potatoes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: POTATOES!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with wishun.

I do on the other hand like the concept and loved the exploration log.

+1 from me, for the idea and the log, and the fact that I would actually love to know more of how this potato universe happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those villagers ate nothing but potatoes for 40+ years?
They must be a bit dumb.
The first guy to grow beets again could have named his own price.
Heck, you could take the donkey on the week long trek to the next town; bring back a tub of ketchup, some vinegar, maybe a bit of fresh fry oil; and lived out the rest of your days like a Siberian rock star!

But anyway, I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bureaucracy, man. You know how it goes. Are there any problems out of Krysovo? No. If you think "there are no problems in this tiny Siberian village out in the frozen-ass end of nowhere" is itself a problem, then you can be the one who investigates it. In your copious free time. Since you obviously don't have enough work to be doing.

Do you want to be the one who tells the boss that you think "everything is just fine" is a problem?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A Russian official reported on 2 June 201█ that Krysovo had made no outside contact in four decades and had no surrounding farmland.

Why did it take 40 years for anyone to notice this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

You somehow made potatoes creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Darn, I'll have to scrap my infinite camel dimension now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anybody ever read future eden?.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, since this has in no way been indicated as being dangerous (if you don't count the exploration log), but that potato in the second photo is really creeping me out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was great and the exploration log totally sold this. A quick note, I would have liked to have seen more detail in the logs though. I'm sure these people would be suffering from some kind of dementia after the cave-in. That would have been neat to see that played out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aw, man. I really wanted that exploration team to make it. This sucks.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The potatoes look delicious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a mix of SCP-871 and SCP-2559-j, but with potatoes. I'm taking away points for originality, but the log was decently written so I'll up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. I was ready to downvote (and actually did at one point due to lack of a rating module), but then I read the log. That was pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Without the log, the article seems a little bland. Within the log, the potatoes seem very bland.

That was about 80% joke. This is dope!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Bag of Infinite Potatoes has an exploration log. I think that's the best thing about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'd think that they would try supplementing their diet at some point. But I'll upvote it as an extension of the exploration log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like to think that it's an alternate dimension where the Keter Cakes got out of hand. Except here they're potatoes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So. Many. Potatoes.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the funniest SCP's I've seen in a long, long time. And it wasn't even a joke one! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We ran out of food rations today. Xander freaked out and started saying we were going to starve, until I told him he was a fucking idiot.

+1. The log really makes it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the log.

"Should've brought an Irishmen"

All my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Flip a coin.

Or, better yet, just +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea whether I should + or -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With exploration log!

I discovered SCP-2559-J about halfway through writing this one. I'm glad you're okay with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… this is basically the Plane of Infinite Kittens.

Only with potatoes.

And not a joke.

…I'm surprisingly OK with this.
